1:
**Part 1 – Personal Biography**

I’m a 22‑year‑old guy living in a modest flat on the outskirts of Almere, just a short bike ride from the city centre. After finishing a mixed track of Havo and VWO, I spent two years in an MBO‑2 programme in electrical installation, and I’m now completing an MBO‑4 apprenticeship as a junior HVAC technician with a local firm. My days usually start with a quick run along the Oostvaardersplassen, then I head to the workshop where I help install climate‑control systems in new housing blocks.  

I share the flat with two housemates—one studying graphic design and the other working as a barista. On weekends we often meet up with my younger sister, who’s finishing her last year of high school, to play board games or watch the latest sci‑fi series. I’m an avid fan of street photography; you’ll often find me wandering the canals at dusk, camera in hand, trying to capture the way the light hits the modern architecture.  

I value reliability and being able to see the tangible results of my work. When I’m not on the job or snapping photos, I volunteer a few evenings a month at a community centre, teaching basic computer skills to seniors.

---

**Part 2 – Political Perspective**

When the 2030 election rolled around, I chose not to cast a ballot. My decision stemmed from a feeling that none of the parties really addressed the everyday challenges I face as a young tradesperson. The long hours at the workshop and the precarious nature of apprenticeship contracts make me wary of promises about large‑scale reforms that seem distant from my workshop floor.  

I’ve seen how budget cuts have affected the community centre where I teach seniors; the reduced funding meant fewer computers and shorter class times. Yet the parties I follow rarely discuss concrete solutions for maintaining local services without vague promises. My experience with the housing market in Almere—rising rents and limited affordable options—has also left me skeptical of policies that talk about “housing for all” without outlining how they’ll be funded or implemented.  

Because of these gaps, I felt my vote wouldn’t translate into meaningful change for people like me. Rather than supporting a party that I’m not convinced will deliver on the specifics that matter to my daily life, I opted out, hoping that my silence might signal the need for clearer, more grounded proposals.
3:
**Part 1 – Personal Biography**

I’m a 58‑year‑old resident of Sliedrecht, a town I’ve called home since I was a teenager. After finishing my Vmbo and a brief stint at the havo‑/vwo‑onderbouw level, I completed an mbo 1 course in administrative assistance. For the past twenty‑four years I’ve worked part‑time at the local municipal office, handling citizen inquiries and processing permits. The job lets me stay close to the community while still having afternoons free to tend to my small backyard garden, where I grow tomatoes, herbs, and a few roses that I sell at the Saturday market.

My husband, Jan, retired from the shipyard a few years ago, and together we look after my mother who lives nearby and needs occasional help with medication and errands. Our two children, both in their thirties, live in Rotterdam and Utrecht; we visit them on holidays and they call often. I’m a regular at the church choir, enjoy knitting blankets for the local senior centre, and every Wednesday I meet a group of friends at the café “De Koffiehoek” for coffee and a game of Scrabble. In my spare time I’m learning to play the accordion, a hobby I picked up after my father’s passing.

---

**Part 2 – Political Perspective**

Living in Sliedrecht and working with everyday citizens has shown me how vital reliable public services are, especially for people like my mother who rely on consistent healthcare and home‑care support. Seeing the challenges my parents face with rising medication costs and the limited options for affordable assisted living made me pay close attention to policies that protect seniors’ financial security. My experience at the municipal office also revealed gaps in pension information and the difficulty many retirees have navigating the system, which often leaves them feeling uncertain about their future.

These practical concerns—steady pension income, accessible health care, and community services that respect the needs of older residents—guide my voting choice. I want a party that prioritises realistic improvements for people in my age group without grandiose promises. That’s why I intend to cast my ballot for 50PLUS in the upcoming election.
